(also known as **Metro Milwaukee** or **Greater Milwaukee**) is a major [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") in southeastern [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), United States, consisting of the city of [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") and some of the surrounding area.

as consisting [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Ozaukee](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_County,_Wisconsin "Washington County, Wisconsin") and [Waukesha](/wiki/Waukesha_County,_Wisconsin "Waukesha County, Wisconsin") counties. With a population of 1.57 million, it is the largest metropolitan area in Wisconsin and the [40th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S. The greater **Milwaukee–Racine–Waukesha combined statistical area** is defined by the U.S. Census Bureau to include [Dodge](/wiki/Dodge_County,_Wisconsin "Dodge County, Wisconsin"), [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson_County,_Wisconsin "Jefferson County, Wisconsin"), [Racine](/wiki/Racine_County,_Wisconsin "Racine County, Wisconsin"), and [Walworth](/wiki/Walworth_County,_Wisconsin "Walworth County, Wisconsin") counties. With a population of 2.05 million, it is the [34th-largest combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") in the country.

The **Milwaukee metropolitan area** (also known as **Metro Milwaukee** or **Greater Milwaukee**) is a major [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") in southeastern [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), United States, consisting of the city of [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") and some of the surrounding area.

The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") defines the **Milwaukee–Waukesha–West Allis metropolitan area** as consisting [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Ozaukee](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_County,_Wisconsin "Washington County, Wisconsin") and [Waukesha](/wiki/Waukesha_County,_Wisconsin "Waukesha County, Wisconsin") counties. With a population of 1.57 million, it is the largest metropolitan area in Wisconsin and the [40th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S. The greater **Milwaukee–Racine–Waukesha combined statistical area** is defined by the U.S. Census Bureau to include [Dodge](/wiki/Dodge_County,_Wisconsin "Dodge County, Wisconsin"), [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson_County,_Wisconsin "Jefferson County, Wisconsin"), [Racine](/wiki/Racine_County,_Wisconsin "Racine County, Wisconsin"), and [Walworth](/wiki/Walworth_County,_Wisconsin "Walworth County, Wisconsin") counties. With a population of 2.05 million, it is the [34th-largest combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") in the country.

The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") defines the Milwaukee Metropolitan area as containing four counties in southeastern Wisconsin: [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee County, Wisconsin") and the three [WOW counties](/wiki/WOW_counties "WOW counties"): [Ozaukee](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_County,_Wisconsin "Washington County, Wisconsin"), and [Waukesha](/wiki/Waukesha_County,_Wisconsin "Waukesha County, Wisconsin").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Metropolitan population of Milwaukee was 1,575,179 in the Census Bureau's 2019 estimate, making it the [39th largest in the United States](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_statistical_areas "List of metropolitan statistical areas").[[8]](#cite_note-2019_est-8)

The city of Milwaukee is the hub of the metropolitan area. The eastern parts of Racine County, eastern parts of Waukesha County, southern part of Ozaukee County, southeastern part of Washington County, and remainder of Milwaukee County are the most urbanized parts of the outlying counties.

The character of the area varies widely. [Mequon](/wiki/Mequon,_Wisconsin "Mequon, Wisconsin"), [Brookfield](/wiki/Brookfield,_Wisconsin "Brookfield, Wisconsin"), and the North Shore ([Fox Point](/wiki/Fox_Point,_Wisconsin "Fox Point, Wisconsin"), [Whitefish Bay](/wiki/Whitefish_Bay,_Wisconsin "Whitefish Bay, Wisconsin"), [River Hills](/wiki/River_Hills,_Wisconsin "River Hills, Wisconsin"), [Shorewood](/wiki/Shorewood,_Wisconsin "Shorewood, Wisconsin"), [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Glendale, Milwaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Brown Deer](/wiki/Brown_Deer,_Wisconsin "Brown Deer, Wisconsin"), and [Bayside](/wiki/Bayside,_Wisconsin "Bayside, Wisconsin")) are more white-collar, while [West Milwaukee](/wiki/West_Milwaukee,_Wisconsin "West Milwaukee, Wisconsin"), [West Allis](/wiki/West_Allis,_Wisconsin "West Allis, Wisconsin"), and [St. Francis](/wiki/St._Francis,_Wisconsin "St. Francis, Wisconsin") are more blue-collar.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Metro Milwaukee draws commuters from outlying areas such as [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and the [Fox Cities](/wiki/Fox_Cities "Fox Cities").
It is part of the [Great Lakes Megalopolis](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Megalopolis "Great Lakes Megalopolis") containing an estimated 54 million people.

### Combined statistical area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Combined statistical area")]

The Milwaukee–Racine–Waukesha [Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/Combined_Statistical_Area "Combined Statistical Area") is made up of the Milwaukee–Waukesha–West Allis [Metropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Metropolitan_Statistical_Area "Metropolitan Statistical Area") (Milwaukee, Waukesha, Washington and Ozaukee counties), the Racine Metropolitan Statistical Area ([Racine County](/wiki/Racine_County,_Wisconsin "Racine County, Wisconsin")), the Beaver Dam Micropolitan Statistical Area ([Dodge County](/wiki/Dodge_County,_Wisconsin "Dodge County, Wisconsin")), the Watertown-Fort Atkinson Micropolitan Area ([Jefferson County](/wiki/Jefferson_County,_Wisconsin "Jefferson County, Wisconsin")), and the Whitewater-Elkorn Micropolitan Area ([Walworth County](/wiki/Walworth_County,_Wisconsin "Walworth County, Wisconsin")) according to the U.S. Census.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Updated definitions released in February 2013 added Dodge, Jefferson and Walworth Counties to the Milwaukee CSA. [Kenosha](/wiki/Kenosha,_Wisconsin "Kenosha, Wisconsin"), despite being just 32 miles from [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") and 50 miles from [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), is included as part of the [Chicago CSA](/wiki/Chicagoland "Chicagoland"), as Kenosha has more residents who commute to the Chicago area. As of the 2019 census estimates, the Milwaukee–Racine–Waukesha [Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/Combined_Statistical_Area "Combined Statistical Area") population was 2,047,966, the largest in Wisconsin and the [33rd largest in the United States](/wiki/List_of_combined_statistical_areas "List of combined statistical areas").[[8]](#cite_note-2019_est-8) The Milwaukee-Racine-Waukesha CSA shares an eastern border with the [Madison MSA](/wiki/Madison_metropolitan_area "Madison metropolitan area") the [Janesville](/wiki/Janesville,_WI "Janesville, WI")-[Beloit](/wiki/Beloit,_WI "Beloit, WI") MSA which in turn are both a part of the Madison CSA.

The city at the center is Milwaukee. Above Milwaukee in the photo is Waukesha. It is a 2012 photo from [Expedition 30](/wiki/Expedition_30 "Expedition 30") of the International Space Station. The line of lights connecting the two cities comes from vehicles and development on and along Bluemound Road and I-94. Due to the angle of the photo, north points rightwards, and west upwards. To the left of Milwaukee along the lakeshore are the cities of Racine, Kenosha, and Waukegan. Kenosha and Waukegan are considered part of the Chicago CSA rather than part of metro Milwaukee. The lights in the far left along the lake are from a portion of Cook County, of which Chicago is the county seat. To the right of Milwaukee are Cedarburg and Grafton. Port Washington is just to the right of Grafton and along the lakeshore. Above Port Washington, the "+" shaped lights are from West Bend. In the far right edge, a small portion of Sheboygan can be seen along the lakeshore.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Politics")]

The city of [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee, Wisconsin") is overwhelmingly [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), while suburban [Milwaukee County](/wiki/Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee County, Wisconsin") was historically politically divided, but has shifted to the left in recent election cycles.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [WOW counties](/wiki/WOW_counties "WOW counties") have long been a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") stronghold for decades, but they have been trending to the left in recent elections. [Dodge County](/wiki/Dodge_County,_Wisconsin "Dodge County, Wisconsin"), [Jefferson County](/wiki/Jefferson_County,_Wisconsin "Jefferson County, Wisconsin"), [Walworth County](/wiki/Walworth_County,_Wisconsin "Walworth County, Wisconsin"), and [Racine County](/wiki/Racine_County,_Wisconsin "Racine County, Wisconsin"), which are located on the outskirts of the metro lean Republican.

Although each county and its various municipalities are self-governing, there is some cooperation in the metropolitan area. The [Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District](/wiki/Milwaukee_Metropolitan_Sewerage_District "Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District") (MMSD) is a state-chartered government agency which serves 28 municipalities in the five counties.

At the same time, some in the area see the need for more consolidation in government services. The [Kettl Commission](/wiki/Kettl_Commission "Kettl Commission") and former Wisconsin Governor [Scott McCallum](/wiki/Scott_McCallum "Scott McCallum") have supported initiatives to do this. However, full consolidation has faced unsubstantiated criticism as a means of diluting [minority](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group") voting power.
